stem: Why does freely diffusing water lose signal after paired diffusion-sensitizing gradients?

options.text: All moving spins leave the body before the second gradient
Random motion prevents complete rephasing by the gradient pair
Diffusion permanently eliminates longitudinal magnetization
The second gradient always has the same polarity as the first

explanation: Stationary spins experience equal and opposite gradient effects and rephase. Diffusing spins change position between lobes, accumulate residual phase dispersion, and lose coherent signal.

stem: Which signal pattern supports true restricted diffusion?

options.text: Low signal on DWI and high signal on ADC
High signal on both DWI and ADC
High signal on high-b-value DWI and low signal on the ADC map
Low signal on both DWI and ADC from every T2-bright lesion

explanation: Restriction limits molecular displacement, preserving high-b DWI signal while producing a low calculated ADC. This combination helps distinguish restriction from T2-related brightness.

stem: What finding suggests that DWI hyperintensity is caused by T2 shine-through rather than restriction?

options.text: The ADC is markedly reduced
The lesion is dark on every T2-weighted image
The b-value is greater than zero
The ADC is not reduced

explanation: A long-T2 lesion can remain bright on DWI even without limited diffusion. Its ADC remains normal or high, whereas true restriction lowers ADC.

stem: What happens when the diffusion b-value is increased?

options.text: Diffusion weighting increases while image SNR generally decreases
Diffusion weighting decreases while SNR always increases
T1 weighting becomes the only contrast mechanism
The ADC calculation becomes independent of signal

explanation: Higher b-values strengthen or lengthen the sensitizing gradients and make signal more dependent on molecular motion. More diffusion-related attenuation also leaves less signal and lowers SNR.

stem: Why are at least two b-values used to calculate an apparent diffusion coefficient?

options.text: One b-value measures T1 and the other measures slice thickness
Signal decay across different diffusion weightings is needed to estimate diffusivity
The second b-value removes the need for spatial encoding
ADC is the arithmetic mean of two field strengths

explanation: ADC is derived from the logarithmic signal change as diffusion weighting changes. A single diffusion-weighted signal cannot separate baseline signal intensity from the attenuation rate.

options.text: Diffusivity is identical in every direction
Only longitudinal relaxation changes with direction
Measured diffusivity depends on the direction of the applied gradients
Water diffusion occurs only when RF is off

explanation: Ordered structures such as white-matter tracts permit easier water motion along some directions than others. Directional diffusion measurements form the basis of diffusion tensor imaging.
